metadata.title: The Math That Proves GPS Is Making You Worse at Navigation

beats.narration_text: Bonjour — this is Liam, in for Bear. Based on the essay 'How GPS Tracking Works — and Why Four Data Points Are Enough to Identify You' by Aditi Shinde, for NortheasternISE / Humanitarians AI. How does GPS actually track you — and what does it know? Two things that sound like they shouldn't be connected: the math that strips your anonymity from location data, and the neuroscience of what turn-by-turn navigation does to your brain. They are connected. Your phone is making you easier to identify and harder to navigate.

props.segment: The Math That Proves GPS Is Making You Worse at Navigation
props.command: How does GPS actually track you — and what does it know?
props.runningText: reading location privacy and spatial cognition research…
output: 4 location data points identify 95% of individuals.
Stripping names/IDs does not anonymize location data.
Turn-by-turn navigation measurably shrinks spatial memory.

beats.narration_text: Four location data points. Home. Work. Gym. Coffee shop. That is enough to uniquely identify 95% of people in a dataset, according to research on large-scale mobility records. Watch what happens as the dots accumulate. One dot — your home neighborhood — thousands of people share it. Add your workplace: the population that shares both is much smaller. Add the gym: smaller still. Add the coffee shop you visit Tuesday mornings: at four data points, 95% of individuals in the dataset have become unique. No names. No phone numbers. No demographic fields. Just four places.

manim.description: City map with dots appearing one at a time. After each dot, an identifiability meter in the corner rises. Dot 1: meter at ~10%. Dot 2: ~40%. Dot 3: ~75%. Dot 4: reaches 95%, highlighted in red. Labels: home, work, gym, coffee shop.

beats.narration_text: The second finding is about your brain. Spatial navigation uses the hippocampus — the region that builds cognitive maps. Active navigation grows it. London taxi drivers famously have enlarged hippocampi from memorizing the city. Turn-by-turn navigation offloads that process to the caudate nucleus instead — the habit-formation system. You stop building a map. You follow a sequence of instructions. The hippocampus is not exercised. Research shows measurable differences in spatial recall between people who navigate actively and those who consistently follow turn-by-turn directions. The carrier knows where you are. You no longer do.

props.artifactHeading: What navigation mode activates which brain region
artifactLines: Active navigation → hippocampus: builds cognitive maps, grows with use.
Turn-by-turn → caudate nucleus: habit sequences, no map built.
London taxi drivers: enlarged hippocampi from memorizing the city.
GPS offloads the map. The carrier keeps it. You don't.

beats.narration_text: K-anonymity is a privacy guarantee: you are anonymous if at least k other people share your combination of attributes. It is the formal standard for anonymized datasets. Location data breaks it fast. In a general population, k-anonymity holds reasonably well for one or two location points. At three points, the number of people sharing your combination falls sharply. At four, 95% of individuals have a unique combination — k equals one. You are the only person in the dataset who visited those four places in that pattern. Name removed. Identity intact.

animated_deck.description: Bar chart showing population sharing a location combination at 1, 2, 3, and 4 data points. Bar drops sharply at each step. At 4 points: bar hits near-zero, threshold line marked at 'k=1 = uniquely identified', 95% annotation.
animated_deck.sparkLine: k=1. You are the only one.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: K-anonymity is a privacy guarantee: you are anonymous if at least k other people share your combination of attributes. It is the formal standard for anonymized

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Four location data points — home, work, gym, coffee shop — identify 95% of individuals in a dataset. Stripping names and IDs does not anonymize location data because the combination of locations itself is unique. Turn-by-turn navigation offloads hippocampal map-building to the caudate nucleus, and measurable spatial recall differences follow. And your carrier continues logging cell tower pings even when you turn GPS off. The phone knows where you are. The math proves who you are.

props.artifactTitle: The Math That Proves GPS Is Making You Worse at Navigation
artifactLines: Four location points identify 95% of individuals — not your phone, you.
Stripping names and IDs doesn't anonymize location data.
Turn-by-turn offloads hippocampal navigation to caudate habit sequences.
Cell tower pings continue even with GPS off.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt — read it with me. 'Build a k-anonymity checker for a location dataset. What's the minimum number of points needed to de-anonymize 50% of users in your test set?' That 50% threshold is the interesting design choice — the research number is 95% at 4 points, but where does the curve turn? At what number of locations does k-anonymity collapse for half your population? Run this on any mobility dataset you can access. The shape of the curve is the result. Liam, in for Bear.
